name: Ahuizotl
description: The Ahuizotl is a fearsome creature from Aztec mythology, a water dog-like beast that lurks in the depths of lakes and rivers. Its name translates to 'thorny water dog' or 'spiny aquatic thing,' a reference to its bristly fur. It is said to possess human-like hands, often located at the ends of its limbs, and a long, prehensile tail with a hand-like appendage at the tip. The Ahuizotl uses this tail to snatch unsuspecting victims from the water's edge, dragging them down to a watery grave. Its cries are said to mimic the sound of a child weeping, luring people closer to the water where it can strike. The Ahuizotl is considered an omen of death, and its appearance is a sign that someone is destined to drown. The victims were believed to be chosen by the water gods, and their bodies would often be found days later, missing their eyes, teeth, and nails. The Ahuizotl served as a warning to stay away from dangerous waters and to respect the power of the aquatic realm. It is a symbol of the unpredictable and deadly forces of nature that were prevalent in the Aztec worldview. Some scholars believe the myth of the Ahuizotl may have originated as a way to explain drownings in the treacherous waterways surrounding Tenochtitlan.
subtitle: Thorny water dog of Aztec legend
appearance: The Ahuizotl is described as a small, dog-like creature, typically black or dark gray in color. Its fur is said to be bristly or thorny. It possesses small ears and smooth skin. Most notably, it has human-like hands at the ends of its limbs, and a long, prehensile tail that also terminates in a hand. Its eyes are small and beady.
habitat: Lakes, rivers, and other bodies of water in central Mexico, particularly around the Valley of Mexico and the former Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan.
behavior: The Ahuizotl is a nocturnal predator that stalks its prey from the depths of the water. It lures victims closer to the water's edge by mimicking the sound of a crying child. Once close enough, it uses its prehensile tail to grab them and drag them underwater. It is said to only prey on those destined to die by drowning, and the water gods are believed to choose its victims.

keyMyths.title: The Drowning of the Unlucky
keyMyths.description: The Ahuizotl selects victims destined to drown, and their bodies are later found with missing eyes, teeth, and nails. This is seen as a sacrifice to the water gods, not a random act of violence by the creature.
keyMyths.source: Various Aztec codices and oral traditions

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
